core_claim: Indirect prompt injection — malicious instructions hidden in content an AI agent reads — gets dangerous when the agent can take actions.

slides.on_slide_copy: THE WEIRDEST AI ATTACK HIDES WHERE YOUR AGENT READS
slides.subline: Prompt injection isn't always typed by the user.
slides.visual_direction: AI browser agent reading a webpage; hidden instruction shadows behind document blocks; electric-blue accent; dark lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: realistic editorial cybersecurity cover, glowing abstract AI agent reading a web page, faint hidden instruction patterns, electric blue, no rendered text, no logos
slides.notes: Reuses the text-free pipeline cover background; React renders the headline on top.

slides.on_slide_copy: Direct injection: the user manipulates the model. Indirect: the content does.
slides.subline: The instruction can come from data, not the chat box.

slides.on_slide_copy: It can live in web pages, emails, documents, images — anywhere the agent reads.
The risk spikes when the AI can act: send email, read files, call APIs.
slides.subline: Reading is one thing. Acting is another.

slides.on_slide_copy: OWASP flags the pattern: injection, sensitive disclosure, excessive agency.
slides.subline: It's a known risk class, not a surprise.

slides.on_slide_copy: Isolate untrusted content. Limit tools. Log actions. Confirm sensitive steps.
slides.subline: Least privilege for agents, not just users.

slides.on_slide_copy: Treat external content like user input wearing a costume.
Would your agent trust a webpage it was told to summarize?
slides.subline: Save this for your next agent design review.

caption:
The weirdest AI attack doesn't get typed into the chat box. It hides in the content your AI agent reads.

That's indirect prompt injection: instructions tucked into a web page, email, document, or image that the model treats as commands. OWASP's Top 10 for LLM Applications lists prompt injection, sensitive-information disclosure, and excessive agency as core risks — and the danger spikes the moment your agent can act (send mail, read files, call APIs) on what it just read.

Defender move: isolate untrusted content from privileged tools, scope agent permissions to the minimum, log every action, and require human confirmation for sensitive steps.

If your agent summarizes a random webpage, should it also be allowed to email your team? Where would you draw that line?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Would your agent trust a webpage it was told to summarize?
alt_text: Slide 1: Dark cinematic cover, an AI browser agent reading a page with hidden instruction shadows, headline about the weirdest AI attack hiding where your agent reads.
Slide 2: Context slide contrasting direct prompt injection (user manipulates the model) with indirect (the content does).
Slide 3: Risk slide listing where injected instructions can live: web pages, emails, documents, images.
Slide 4: Mechanism slide noting the risk spikes when the AI can act — send email, read files, call APIs.
Slide 5: Failure-point slide referencing the OWASP pattern: prompt injection, sensitive disclosure, excessive agency.
Slide 6: Defense slide: isolate untrusted content, limit tools, log actions, confirm sensitive steps.
Slide 7: Takeaway slide: treat external content like user input wearing a costume.
Slide 8: Call-to-action slide asking whether your agent should trust a webpage it was told to summarize, with a save and comment prompt.

sources.supports: Prompt Injection, Sensitive Information Disclosure, and Excessive Agency are listed risk entries for LLM applications.
